trajectory.thought: Element A reacts with water at high temperatures to form a binary compound B. A binary compound contains only two elements, so B contains only A and oxygen (from water, with H₂ released) or A and hydrogen. Since B then reacts with NaOH/Br₂ to form compound C which contains two metals (Na from NaOH, and A), element A must be a metal. The binary compound B formed from a metal reacting with water at high temperature would be a metal oxide (the metal reacts with steam: metal + H₂O → metal oxide + H₂). This confirms element A is a metal.

Compound B (binary compound of element A) reacts with NaOH/Br₂. Br₂ in alkaline medium acts as the oxidant, oxidizing element A to a higher oxidation state. The NaOH provides the alkaline medium and Na⁺ ions, which become incorporated into compound C (a sodium salt). This is consistent with Bi₂O₃ being oxidized by Br₂ in NaOH to form NaBiO₃, where Bi goes from +3 to +5 oxidation state.

Compound C is ternary (three elements) with two metals in 1:1 ratio. The reaction involves NaOH as a reagent, so sodium (Na) is one metal. Element A is the other metal. The third element must be oxygen (O), since the reaction involves NaOH and an oxide (B). Therefore C has the general formula NaAO_m, where Na:A = 1:1.
The general formula for compound C is NaAO_m, where Na contributes 23 g/mol, element A contributes M_A g/mol, and m oxygen atoms contribute 16m g/mol. Therefore M_C = 23 + M_A + 16m.
From the formula derived in step 5: M_A = (17.158 + 11.936·m) / 0.254

Bismuth (Bi) reacts with steam at high temperatures to form Bi₂O₃ (bismuth(III) oxide), a binary compound. Bi₂O₃ then reacts with Br₂ in concentrated NaOH solution, where Br₂ oxidizes Bi from +3 to +5, forming sodium bismuthate (NaBiO₃). The reaction is: Bi₂O₃ + 2Br₂ + 6NaOH → 2NaBiO₃ + 4NaBr + 3H₂O. This confirms compound C is NaBiO₃.

All conditions have been verified in the plan. NaBiO₃ satisfies every constraint: RFM = 280 > 230, %Bi = 209/280 × 100% = 74.6%, two metals (Na and Bi) in 1:1 ratio, ternary compound (Na, Bi, O), anhydrous, and NaBiO₃ is a well-known strong oxidiser.
